Front: In ML/AI hiring, what does a depth interview probe that a breadth interview does not, and why run both?
Back: Depth probes whether expertise goes to first principles (can they keep answering "why" in their specialty). Breadth probes range across the field (can they adapt and collaborate across subfields). Run both because passing one says nothing about the other; together they reveal the candidate's shape and catch deep-but-narrow or broad-but-shallow.

Front: AICE framework: what distinguishes Influence from Execution?
Back: Execution = your ability to deliver, especially under limited time/resources (your own output reaching the finish line). Influence = leverage / force multiplication: how far your impact reaches beyond your own hands, via docs, tools, mentoring, and setting direction that changes how OTHERS work.

Front: AICE: give a clean example of high Influence but low Execution.
Back: Someone whose designs and technical direction align the whole team, but who personally cannot reliably ship on a deadline. Great ideas, weak at landing them. (High execution / low influence = the prolific solo shipper measured in PRs who never raises anyone else's output.)

Front: Why are evals essential for ML/LLM products, and how do they differ from unit tests?
Back: A unit test is a deterministic binary assertion (input X must give exact output Y). ML/LLM outputs are probabilistic and open-ended, so you grade quality across a DATASET using metrics (accuracy, faithfulness, relevance). Main day-to-day value: regression detection. Without evals you cannot tell if a prompt/model/data change helped or hurt; you ship on vibes.

Front: Product of Array Except Self: why does answer[i] = (product left of i) x (product right of i)?
Back: Every element other than nums[i] is either strictly left of i or strictly right of i, with no third option. So multiplying the left product by the right product includes every element exactly once except nums[i], which belongs to neither side. This excludes i with no division.

Front: Product of Array Except Self: what seeds the first prefix slot and last suffix slot, and why?
Back: 1, the multiplicative identity (empty product). Index 0 has nothing to its left and the last index has nothing to its right, so each starts at 1. Seeding with 0 would zero out the whole answer. (Analog: prefix-SUM problems seed with 0.)

Front: Why is the math.prod(nums) // nums[i] one-liner wrong for Product of Array Except Self? Use [1,2,0,4].
Back: Two independent reasons: (1) the problem forbids division; (2) it breaks on zeros. Correct output for [1,2,0,4] is [0,0,8,0]. total = 0, so 0 // nums[i]; index 2 is 0 // 0 which throws ZeroDivisionError, and that index is the only nonzero answer.
